issue_title: What happens when a WebSocket Server is publishing faster than a Client?
issue_body:
## Long story short
What happens if the client is not consuming the events coming from the server? Can a malicious client jam the server?

Those two links might be relevant to understand the problem:
 - [what happens when tcp/udp server is publishing faster than client is consuming?](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1997691/what-happens-when-tcp-udp-server-is-publishing-faster-than-client-is-consuming)
 - [Write completion flow control](http://www.lenholgate.com/blog/2008/07/write-completion-flow-control.html)
 - [TCP flow control and asynchronous writes](http://www.serverframework.com/asynchronousevents/2011/06/tcp-flow-control-and-asynchronous-writes.html)

I dug in the code, the closest thing I've found about handling buffers is the [`drain`](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/d08651ce281abed518c0bcefedd03cfc72b07a74/aiohttp/http_writer.py#L118) method. 

*Side note*: I'm not familiar with the `aiohttp` code base, but I noticed that `drain` is a coroutine that is eventually called by `send_str`, but `send_str` is not a coroutine, is it a problem?

## Expected behaviour

The server should stop writing to that specific WebSocket, or overwrite the buffer with the new data.

## Actual behaviour

I've no idea.

## Steps to reproduce

### Client code
After connecting to the server, the client starts twiddling its thumbs.

```python
# Client

import asyncio
import aiohttp


async def run(session):
    async with session.ws_connect('http://localhost:8080/') as ws:
        while True:
            await asyncio.sleep(1000)

session = aiohttp.ClientSession()
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(run(session))
```

### Server code
On the other side, the server works hard to deliver it's extremely important kilobyte of `a`s
```python
# Server

import asyncio
from aiohttp import web


async def websocket_handler(request):
    ws = web.WebSocketResponse()
    await ws.prepare(request)

    s = 'a' * 1024

    i = 0
    while True:
        i += 1
        print('send ', len(s) * i)
        ws.send_str(s)
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)

    return ws


app = web.Application()
app.router.add_get('/', websocket_handler)
web.run_app(app)
```
## Your environment

Python 3.6+aiohttp-2.0.7 on a Ubuntu machine.

You're supposed to be able to do things like this:

```
<greeting>= (howdy | good morning) {hi};
```

So if i say, "howdy | good morning", it should return the text "hi". This does not appear to work.
Any reason why on PyPi we have ``2018.1.18`` but the package version says ``2018.01.18``? This makes it hard to write tests that after install what we installed is actually what we have.

issue_title: Run script on renewal of certificate
issue_body:
The preferred way to use letsencrypt certificates, is pointing applications directly into `/etc/letsencrypt/live`.  However, for several reasons (see below), this might not work and some custom steps need to be taken to install a certificate for an application.  An admin should be able to install a script which is run on the renewal of a certificate to install it.

A hypothetical implementation could work as follows

    letsencrypt -d mydomain --on-renewal=/usr/local/bin/install-cert-for-lighttpd

With the script `/usr/local/bin/install-cert-for-lighttpd` containing, for instance

    cat "/etc/letsencrypt/live/$1/privkey.pem" "/etc/letsencrypt/live/$1/cert.pem" > /etc/lighttpd/ssl/$1.pem
    chown www-data:www-data /etc/lighttpd/ssl/$1.pem
    service lighttpd reload

Some reasons I can think of for a script are:

1. The applications requires that the certificate and key in a modified format.   See  #1201
2. The application does not run as root.  Thus, we need to make a copy of the certificate only readable by the user as which the application is running.  This is the case for `inspircd`.

issue_title: Move development-environment into a repository of it's own.
issue_body:
The current git-repository (and thus the distribution) contains the *complete* test environment, including testing-tools, docker- and vagrant-files, examples, the complete documentation.

**This is contrary to the security best-practice of installing only the minimal requires software on production systems.**

It may even contradict ISO 27002 which recommends separating test and production environment.

On Fedora 22 running nginx server:

```
(my_env)# ./bin/letsencrypt -d example.com -d www.example.com
```
Then go to the installer window. Enter email, agree,  then it finished with this error:
```
 Unable to run the command: apache2ctl configtest  
```
**apache2ctl** does not exist, but **apachectl** indeed.

issue_title: letsencrypt requires root for "plugins" command, Manual Authenticator, generic plugins
issue_body:
Since https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/pull/843 was not pulled in, we need ways of allowing people to run the client without root for the following cases:
- `letsencrypt plugins`, possibly other subcommands
- `letsencrypt -a manual auth` or anything else using Manual Authenticator: paranoid people don't want to run our code as `sudo` - we should make it easy for them to do that
- in general, plugins might not require `sudo` at all: #496 is an example, but we can't anticipate for what reasons 3rd party plugins would not require `root`, so we need a generic solution.

Assigning to @pde.
The library is not working without the `requests` library. Hence, it should be added as dependency.

issue_title: consumer_lag statistic does not reset after rebalance operation
issue_body:
Description
===========
`consumer_lag` statistic does not reset after rebalance operation. It maintains the previous value when the partition was still assigned.

================

Run a producer and consumer (part of a cgrp). Allow message to be processed by the consumer and add another consumer. Observe the statistics outputted from both consumers. You'll notice that the `consumer_lag` maintains the last seen value rather than resetting back to zero when partitions are revoked.

issue_title: The tarball in 1.1 is missing test data

There is currently no inverse to the `isoformat()` function. If you *know* you've got an iso8601 date, the logic is actually *much* simpler than the current parser logic, but because `isoformat()` outputs a format not compatible with even Python 3.6's `strptime()` (shamefully), it's some pretty low hanging fruit.

issue_title: Parser error with unambiguous date in year 31
issue_body:
I started converting [this test](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/test/test_parser.py#L33) over to a hypothesis-based property test and found the following failure:

```python
from dateutil.parser import parse
from datetime import datetime

dt = datetime(31, 12, 30)
dstr = dt.strftime('%B.%04Y.%d')    # December.0031.30
parse(dt.strftime('%B.%04Y.%d'))     # datetime(30, 12, 31, 0, 0)
```

Pretty low priority, but related to #293. Here's the full hypothesis test that generates these examples:

```python
from datetime import datetime, time
import itertools

from hypothesis.strategies import integers, dates
from hypothesis import given

import pytest

from dateutil.parser import parserinfo, parse


def _get_ybd_formats():
    # If we have a 4-digit year, a non-numeric month (abbreviated or not),
    # and a day (1 or 2 digits), then there is no ambiguity as to which
    # token is a year/month/day.  This holds regardless of what order the
    # terms are in and for each of the separators below.
    try:
        datetime.now().strftime('%-d')
        PLATFORM_HAS_DASH_D = True
    except ValueError:
        PLATFORM_HAS_DASH_D = False

    seps = ['-', ' ', '/', '.']

    year_tokens = ['%04Y']
    month_tokens = ['%b', '%B']
    day_tokens = ['%d']

    if PLATFORM_HAS_DASH_D:
        day_tokens.append('%-d')

    prods = itertools.product(year_tokens, month_tokens, day_tokens)
    perms = [y for x in prods for y in itertools.permutations(x)]
    unambig_fmts = [sep.join(perm) for sep in seps for perm in perms]

    return unambig_fmts


@pytest.mark.parser
@pytest.mark.parametrize('fmt', _get_ybd_formats())
@given(dt=dates())
def test_ybd(dt, fmt):
    dt = datetime.combine(dt, time(0))  # Make this a datetime
    dstr = dt.strftime(fmt)
    res = parse(dstr)
    assert dt == res
```

In the end we may also want to change `_get_ybd_formats` over to a strategy, because I think repeating dozens of hypothesis tests separately is expensive.

I have an app that uses Telethon to listen to messages from some big channels (2k+ subscribers) and relay them. Since version 0.17 I've been noticing some delays in it. So I made the code below to test if the delay was on Telethon and discovered that telethon has been taking over 20s average to run the callback function. 


```
    client = TelegramClient('session', api_id, api_hash, update_workers=1, spawn_read_thread=False).start(phone)

    try:
        client.get_me()
    except RuntimeError:
        print('Couldn\'t login to Telegram. Exiting...')
        sys.exit(0)

    #channel= client.get_input_entity('A BIG CHANNEL')
    

    @client.on(events.NewMessage(chats=channel, incoming=True))
    def callback(event):
        print('Message at {} UTC'.format(event.message.date))
        print('Delay was {}s\n'.format((datetime.utcnow() - event.message.date).seconds))

    print('Listening for messages...')
    client.idle()
```

OUT:
```
Message at 2018-02-28 03:50:33 UTC
Delay was 52s

Message at 2018-02-28 03:54:23 UTC
Delay was 26s
```


When listening from a test channel with just me as a subscriber, there is no delay. I've been thinking about rolling back my Telethon version, but maybe you guys can help me with it.

### What went wrong
`client.is_user_authorized()` opens additional TLS connection to 163.172.167.189:443 and sends some data.

### What I've done
Removed `username.session` file, started capture in wieshark and run client:

```python
from telethon import TelegramClient

API_ID = 12345
API_HASH = '<hash>'

client = TelegramClient('username', API_ID, API_HASH)
client.connect()
print(client.is_user_authorized())
```

### More information
Part of wireshark dump:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1857617/30802221-72735c30-a1ee-11e7-83e4-e470d7af1671.png)

If acces to 163.172.167.189 is blocked (for example with `iptables -A OUTPUT -d 163.172.167.189 -j DROP`) then `is_user_authorized` hangs for ~1 minute but returns without error.

issue_title: Question about fields.Nested  and APISpec.definition() call order
issue_body:
What is correct way to handle nested schemas with apispec? For example I have something like that

```python
class BarSchema(Schema):
    baz = fields.String()


class FooSchema(Schema):
    bars = fields.Nested(BarSchema, many=True)


spec.definition('Foo', spec=FooSchema)
spec.definition('Bar', spec=BarSchema)
spec.to_dict()
```

Generated definition for Foo includes Bar as nested object instead of referencing existing definition. Changing order of schemas in spec helps
```python
spec.definition('Bar', spec=BarSchema)
spec.definition('Foo', spec=FooSchema)
```
but what about situations with circular dependencies when FooSchema nests BarSchema and vice versa?

Possible feature: add a `Field` parameter that would make the field active for validation/deserialization but not appear in serialization output.

This came up in a discussion here: https://github.com/marshmallow-code/flask-marshmallow/issues/5#issuecomment-65887841 (though we used the term "write-only", which is a misnomer).

Related to #61 . 

EDIT: Fix URL to comment.

I have a boolean field that is required only if some other field is filled in (I cannot specify `required=True` on the dependent field).  So I've written a schema validator to check: `if first_field is True then second_field is required`.

This dependent boolean field is not something that can default to `true` or `false`.  If a user does not fill in the boolean field, I would like Marshmallow to throw a validation error.  However, because null booleans automatically deserialize to `False`, I cannot check if the boolean came in as `None` or `False`.

Is there a way to prevent this behavior?

If schema is declared like this:
```python
class ChildSchema(Schema):
    foo = fields.Method('dump_foo')
    def dump_foo(self, obj):
        print 'context is', self.context
  
class ParentSchema(Schema):
    child = fields.List(fields.Nested(ChildSchema))
```
Then `context` is saved on first call and ignored on further calls:
```python
>>> ParentSchema(context={'bar': 1}).dump({'child': [{}]})
context is {'bar': 1}
MarshalResult(...)
>>> ParentSchema(context={'baz': 2}).dump({'child': [{}]})
context is {'bar': 1}
MarshalResult(...)
>>> ParentSchema().dump({'child': [{}]})
context is {'bar': 1}
MarshalResult(...)
```

It is because `copy.deepcopy(self._declared_fields)` doesn't copy `List.container` field and schema with `context` is cached inside `Nested` field and saved inside `_declared_fields`.

issue_title: In Python 3, ApiKey list in admin is not useful
issue_body: The ApiKey list in the admin isn't useful under Python 3 because ApiKey only implements __unicode__. Other models in `tastypie.models` use the `@python_2_unicode_compatible` decorator, but for some reason ApiKey does not. I'll prepare and submit the 2-line fix.

       (= |main::j@3| (+ 1 |main::j@2|))
       (= |main::k@3| (+ (- 1) |main::k@2|)))))
(assert (let ((a!1 (<= (+ (+ |main::j@2| (* (- 1) |main::k@2|)) |main::n@3|) 0))
      (a!2 (<= (+ (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@2|) |main::k@2|) |main::n@3|) 2))
      (a!3 (<= (+ (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@2|) (* (- 1) |main::k@2|)) |main::n@3|)
               0)))
  (and (= __INITIAL_LOCATION 1)
       true
       (<= (+ |main::j@2| |main::n@3|) 1)
       (<= (+ |main::k@2| |main::n@3|) 2)
       (<= (+ |main::j@2| |main::k@2|) 1)
       (<= |main::n@3| 1)
       (<= (+ (+ |main::j@2| |main::k@2|) |main::n@3|) 2)
       a!1
       a!2
       (<= |main::j@2| 0)
       (<= |main::k@2| 1)
       (<= (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@2|) |main::k@2|) 1)
       a!3
       (<= (+ |main::j@2| (* (- 1) |main::k@2|)) 0)
       (<= (* (- 1) |main::k@2|) 0)
       (<= (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@2|) (* (- 1) |main::k@2|)) 0)
       (<= (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@2|) |main::n@3|) 1)
       (<= (+ (* (- 1) |main::k@2|) |main::n@3|) 0)
       (<= (* (- 1) |main::j@2|) 0))))
(assert true)
(maximize (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@3|) (* (- 1) |main::k@3|) |main::n@3|))
(set-option :opt.optsmt_engine basic)
(set-option :opt.priority box)
(check-sat)
```

I get the bound "oo".

It is obviously incorrect by looking at the query, but it's even more obvious if we ask Z3 itself whether 
`(= (+ (* (- 1) |main::j@3|) (* (- 1) |main::k@3|) |main::n@3|) 10)` (or any number greater than `0` in general) is satisfiable, because the bound should be indeed zero.

The slice of the query essentially resolves to "maximize -j'-k'+n' s.t. k' = k -1 /\ j'=j+1 /\ n'=n /\ -j-k+n<=0 ", which does not change the bound.

issue_title: [Z3py] to_smt2() does not work with FPA
issue_body:
I wanted to use `to_smt2()` to generate a testcase for a segfault that I get with commit 7eec68c but it looks like it does not generate valid code. The following example is unrelated to the segfault but illustrates the issues that I ran into:

```
$ python
>>> from z3 import *
>>> s = Solver()
>>> s.add(FP('x', Float32()) == 5.0)
>>> with open('out.txt', 'w') as f:
...     f.write(s.to_smt2())
$ cat out.txt
; benchmark generated from python API
(set-info :status unknown)
(declare-fun x () (FloatingPoint 8 24))
(assert
 (= x (_ fp.numeral @0)))
(check-sat)
$ z3 -smt2 out.txt
(error "line 3 column 33: invalid sort, symbol, '_' or '(' expected")
(error "line 5 column 4: unknown constant x")
sat
```

I'm not very familiar with the SMT2 syntax but it looks like `(FloatingPoint 8 24)` should change to `(_ FloatingPoint 8 24)` and  `@0` should be declared somewhere.

Thanks :)

As originally discussed in #1630, the current `buffer<T>` implementation can possibly be improved upon.

I would like to submit this analysis for your consideration, so that an eventual pull request matches your expectations.

### Properties of the Current Implementation
- The current implementation restricts its capacity to 2^31, which effectively saves 8 bytes on 64 bit machines
  - The restriction 2^31 follows from how `buffer::expand` works and from `buffer::INITIAL_SIZE` always being a power of 2
  - Saving 8 bytes happens because `buffer::m_pos` and `buffer::m_capacity` should otherwise be `std::size_t`, which requires 4 bytes more on 64 bit targets. For most compilers, padding should not be introduced in either case.
- Relies on the user deciding whether destructors should be explicitly called (`buffer`'s `CallDestructors` template argument).
  - Sometimes the user may decide in a way that is inconsistent with C++ rules (the destructor may not be trivial in the C++ sense, but the use may decide to not call it anyway)
  - Sometimes the user may be wrong not just in the strict sense (cf. [`sbuffer`](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/6938c76950f21c0e7bb958376604cb0fc4811784/src/util/buffer.h#L260-L265), [`smt::coeff_expr`](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/6938c76950f21c0e7bb958376604cb0fc4811784/src/smt/theory_arith.h#L991), [`smt::theory_arith<Ext>::display_coeff_exprs(std::ostream&, sbuffer<coeff_expr> const&) const`](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/6938c76950f21c0e7bb958376604cb0fc4811784/src/smt/theory_arith.h#L1129) considering [`~rational`](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/6938c76950f21c0e7bb958376604cb0fc4811784/src/util/rational.h#L67)).
  - Maybe somewhat surprisingly, this optimization can easily be done by the compiler: If `~T()` is trivial, [the loop](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/6938c76950f21c0e7bb958376604cb0fc4811784/src/util/buffer.h#L49-L53) becomes an empty loop over a pointer range, which can then be removed, (and so forth)
  - The same problem also exists in the opposite direction, some types which do not need to be destroyed (e.g. `bool` and `char`) exist in both specializations, which at least introduces unnecessary types
- Assumes that all types it is instantiated with are [trivially copyable](http://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/types/is_trivially_copyable) by moving them with `memcpy` by default.
  - Often quite fast
  - Often OK in practice, as destructors are not called on the old objects => usually works in practice as long as the value of `this` is irrelevant
  - Questionable C++
- Basically ignores alignment
  - On common 32 bit and 64 bit machines, `m_initial_buffer` is still pointer aligned, which mostly works (practically speaking, SSE may not be used on buffers)
  - Can be easily fixed with pretty much zero overhead (`alignas(alignof(T))`)
- Semantically closely related to [`vector`](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/6938c76950f21c0e7bb958376604cb0fc4811784/src/util/vector.h#L41-L42), which is basically a `buffer` with `INITIAL_SIZE=0`.

### Target Properties
- Be correct
- Performance (memory and speed) should (at least) not degrade significantly unless required for correctness
- The implementation should become as standards compliant as possible w/o losing performance
- Use template mechanisms to ensure that destructors and move constructors are called where necessary
  - Possibly rely on the optimizer to deal with this
- The type of `m_pos` and `m_capacity` (or their equivalents) should be templatized as in `vector`
- Use `realloc` for improved performance where possible?
- Unify with `vector`?

### List of Instantiations
By forcing that `T` is one of a list of known types and iteratively adding types to this list, I have come up with a hopefully complete list of instantiations. The result [can be seen here](https://github.com/danielschemmel/z3/blob/72c79a10c63334399460f0763c07f7e044344179/src/util/buffer.h#L50-L94).

### Other Notes
Due to its internal structure, accessing the buffer requires chasing a pointer - no matter if it is still the initial buffer or not. If this were to be replaced with a branch over the capacity, the object size could be reduced by 8 bytes on a 64 bit machine. If it is guaranteed that the initial buffer is always large enough, this is inefficient in either case.

issue_title: Add and/or document a way to run commands from a file
issue_body:
`sqlcmd` provides a [`:r`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/tools/sqlcmd-utility#sqlcmd-commands) meta-command to run commands from a file in the current session. `psql` spells it [`\i`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/static/app-psql.html). Does this feature exist in `mssql-cli`? I tried both `:r` and (for the heck of it) `\i` to no avail, though the latter did pop up a tantalizing file browser. 🤔

```
$ mssql-cli -d foo
Version: 0.8.0
Mail: sqlcli@microsoft.com
Home: http://github.com/dbcli/mssql-cli
foo>:r bar.sql
Msg 102, Level 15, State 1, Line 1
Incorrect syntax near ':'.

issue_title: Trying to use one function of mjlib
issue_body:
Hi, 

I have doubt, can anyone please help?

I want to use the mj_fullM function of mjlib. (line 172 and 173). This is commented. So in my forked version of mujoco-py, I tried to add a wrapper for same as follows:


Line 172 and 173 in mjlib
```
mjlib.mj_fullM.argtypes = [POINTER(MJMODEL), POINTER(c_double), POINTER(c_double)]
mjlib.mj_fullM.restype = None
```

in mjcore.py

```
def fullM(self):
        array_length = self.nv*self.nv*100
        fullqM = np.zeros((array_length))
        mjlib.mj_fullM(self.ptr, fullqM.astype(np.double).ctypes.data_as(POINTER(c_double)), self.data.qM.astype(np.double).ctypes.data_as(POINTER(c_double)))
        return fullqM
```
But on calling in my main:

`myObj.model.fullM()`, I get back just a **bunch of zeros**. While `myObj.model.data.qM` does have the values in it.

In the mujoco library the mj_fullM function converts the reduced inertia matrix to the complete inertia matrix.

What am I doing wrong? Any suggestion?

issue_title: Getting "E0301 04:53:39.120702 Reloader directory_watcher.py:241: xyz updated updated  even though the current file is zyx
issue_body:
Does TensorBoard support multiple processes creating "tf.summary.FileWriter(logdir," objects with same logdir?

I found this causes error messages like this

```E0301 04:53:39.120702 Reloader directory_watcher.py:241] File /efs/runs/psbench3/events.out.tfevents.1519880018.ip-192-168-5-194 updated even though the current file is /efs/runs/psbench3/events.out.tfevents.1519880018.ip-192-168-5-221
```

In one case, the tensorboard showed things normally (version TensorBoard 1.5.1), in another case, it failed to show any events from the updated files (version 1.5.0a20171222).

In the later case, either restarting TensorBoard, or removing one of the FileWriters made things work

In the file `gcdt/gcdt_defaults.py` there is some default configreader config specified in `CONFIG_READER_CONFIG`:

```python
CONFIG_READER_CONFIG = {

    'lookups': ['secret', 'ssl', 'stack', 'baseami', 'acm'],

```

There are different options I can think of:
* still have those default lookups, but do not hard fail if the plugin is not installed
* have a separate package, that contains these glomex defaults

issue_title: certificates should be installed to /var/lib/letsencrypt, not /etc/letsencrypt
issue_body:
Please consider installing the certificates to /var/lib/letsencrypt/{live,archive,...}.  They are automatically generated and thus don't count as configuration files (IMHO).

Using /var/lib/letsencrypt instead of /etc also allows running the letsencrypt client without write permissions to /etc, for example when mounting /etc read-only or when using systemd with its ProtectSystem=full option.

See also the [File Hierarchy Standard](http://www.pathname.com/fhs/pub/fhs-2.3.html#ETCHOSTSPECIFICSYSTEMCONFIGURATION) which defines /etc as "static", that is "files that do not change without system administrator intervention".

issue_title: Chunked Upload fails on space characters in file name
Refactoring - Invert/Rename RaidenAPI and Raiden class
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

The current Raiden architecture of the Raiden God Object having a Raiden API class inside it and being called by yet another API (the REST API), is quite convoluted and confusing. After our discussions in the Andalucia Retreat we came up with the following changes to the architecture.



## Tasklist

- Move what is now the `RaidenAPI `class outside of the the Raiden God object and rename it to Raiden.
- What is now the Raiden God Object will be renamed to `RaidenCore`.
- The new Raiden object (internal API) will only be accepting binary and each interface, like the REST API or the CLI will have to take care of encoding on their own.

This is what the architecture will look like after the refactoring is done, but before restartable tasks are merged. 
![img_20170323_115956](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1658405/24245676/17f6d596-0fc4-11e7-9316-4e2ce09fd878.jpg)

issue_title: Missing return in RestAPI connect() and leave()
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Both `RestAPI.connect()` and `RestAPI.leave()` are missing `return` statements to return a HTTP response.

## Solution

Return appropriate values from both methods.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Return Rest response from `RestAPI.connect()`
- [ ] Return Rest response from `RestAPI.leave()`

Right now sos records signatures at step and task levels. When we work on a step with long running scripts, it might be helpful to have action level signature so that long-running scripts can be more easily mixed with other actions. in my case, adding another `run` action after a `run` action running `STAR` causes the whole `STAR` command to be re-executed.

issue_title: When a worker fails to start, its pid should be removed from local scheduler.
issue_body:
### Describe the problem
<!-- Describe the problem clearly here. -->
Currently, when a worker fails to start, we don't remove its pid from local scheduler. As a result, local scheduler won't start any new workers afterwards, because it think there's still a pending worker to be registered, see https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/19b743c84b6007ad247e6d00807b3530c6dbc09a/src/local_scheduler/local_scheduler_algorithm.cc#L917
In some cases, this could make the whole system hang forever.

### Reproduce the problem
To reproduce this issue, one can add the following code in the beginning of `default_worker.py`:
```
import commands
existing_workers = int(commands.getstatusoutput("ps aux | grep 'ray/workers/default_worker' | grep -v grep | grep -v vim | wc -l")[1].strip()) - 2
if existing_workers == 3: # the 4th worker happens to fail
    raise Exception("")
print('Worker %d started' % (existing_workers + 1))
```
And then run this script:
```
# this script needs at least 4 workers
import ray
import time
@ray.remote
def g(x):
    return x
@ray.remote
def f(x):
    time.sleep(1)
    return ray.get(g.remote(x))
ray.init(num_workers=0)
res = []
for i in range(3):
    time.sleep(0.1)
    res.append(f.remote(i))
print(ray.get(res))
```
### Solution
To fix this issue, I'm thinking of checking all child_pids' existence before `dispatch_tasks`. But I'd also like to hear if you have better ideas before starting working on the fix. Thank you.

issue_title: Remove hack for placing actors and remove GPU bookkeeping redundancy.
issue_body:
Currently, there is some redundancy in how we do bookkeeping of GPU resources.

First of all, the local scheduler on a given machine is responsible for reserving GPUs for the tasks and actors that run on that machine. It keeps track of which GPUs are in use by which workers. It releases those GPUs when the workers are done with them or when the worker dies. The local scheduler keeps track of the **ground truth** about who is allowed to use which GPUs on a given machine.

However, we also do some heuristic bookkeeping in the primary Redis shard to keep track of which GPUs are in use. This information is **not the ground truth**. It is just used to help with actor placement. The reason that we currently do this is that when a driver or worker creates an actor, the driver or worker that creates the actor chooses which local scheduler to assign the actor to. The placement decision does not go through any local or global schedulers. So if the actor requires GPUs, the worker or driver creating the actor needs to know where it can find some GPUs. Hence it checks the primary Redis shard, chooses a local scheduler with enough GPUs, and updates the primary Redis shard (as a Redis transaction).

For various reasons, the information in Redis is not perfectly in sync with the ground truth.
- It doesn't reflect any information about non-actor tasks that are using GPUs.
- If an actor dies, it may not properly update Redis until the driver finishes.
- If an actor exits naturally (e.g., it's handle goes out of scope), it does not update Redis (fixed by #1088).

FWIW, this hack seems to have worked pretty well so far, e.g., we haven't had issues with actor placement (that I'm aware of) other than #1088. But it adds complexity, so we should probably fix this.

cc @stephanie-wang @atumanov @ericl

issue_title: EventLoop doesn't provide Reader a way to terminate execution elegantly
issue_body:
### Problem
The code in the [EventLoop class](https://github.com/alphatwirl/alphatwirl/blob/v0.9.x/alphatwirl/loop/EventLoop.py#L25-L33) handles the reader (typically a composite reader) and provides it with events from the tree(s) by:
1. Call `reader.begin()` method
2. For each event:
    a. Call `reader.event()`
3. Call `reader.end()`

In each case, the return of each of `reader`'s methods is ignored.  This makes it awkward for a reader to tell the event loop to terminate early, or not run at all in the case of `begin()`. `begin()` can raise an exception of course, but in some cases that is more extreme than really needed.

### Solution
Have the return of each calll to reader `begin`, `event`, and `end` checked, and execution halted if the return does not evaluate to true.

### Specific example
I'm running over a large heppy dataset and including the existing RA1 AlphaTools sequence as a single Reader.  The heppy dataset is being split up using AlphaTwirl and submitted to the Bristol htcondor batch system.  One of the datasets in this input is not supposed to be handled by AlphaTools and AlphaTools knows this, so it would normally just ignore it, however this causes an exception to be raised crashing the batch job, which is then re-submitted.  To enable things to finish elegantly, I would prefer to be able to signal to the EventLoop that this jobs should not proceed in the `begin()` method of the reader that wraps AlphaTools.

issue_title: endpoint not being tracked still shows up in Version Usage
issue_body:
i just removed the /validate endpoint from the ones being tracked. 
however, it still shows up in the Version Usage. 

is this right?

Do we have the flexibility to do our own loss function using python? 
I have written a custom loss function that combine cross entropy and a custom regularization term using y_ and output of some dense layers. But I haven't figured out how can I feed the value of a batch of sample into my custom regularization term.

As of Version 13.1, PyPSA only considers investment in one horizon, e.g. for a single year. We'd like to implement investment over multiple horizons simultaneously, e.g. for 2020, 2025, 2030, 2035, etc., to optimise investments dynamically over several years e.g. under tightening CO2 constraints. The objective function then becomes the discounted net present value (NPV) of energy system costs. Other open models such as [OSeMOSYS](http://www.osemosys.org/), [Genesys](http://www.genesys.rwth-aachen.de/), [DIETER](http://www.diw.de/dieter) (I think) and [SWITCH](https://github.com/switch-model/switch) do this already; we can copy their implementations and avoid reinventing the wheel.
On the data side:

i) We need a second index for snapshots that gives the investment year, e.g.
snapshots = [(2020, "windy day"), (2020, "sunny day"), (2030, "windy day"), (2030, "sunny day")]
The representative weather for each investment year may change with the year (e.g. due to climate change); the number of representative snapshots for each investment year may also change.

ii) We need different CO2/renewable constraints for different investment years.

iii) For assets, we need to take account of build years and lifetimes for generation, storage and transmission assets, e.g. we need additional fields

name, build year, life time, cost
wind-in-2020, 2020, 20, 1200
wind-in-2025, 2025, 20, 1000
wind-in-2030, 2030, 25, 900

Then in the code we need to activate assets in the constraints depending on whether they are around in the investment year or not.
We also need to take account of the discount rate as a pre-factor to get the NPV for each investment year.
Perhaps this should be linked to the issue of separating the snapshot_weighting from the duration of each snapshot, which was discussed on the PyPSA mailing list before.
BEWARE A): The final investment horizon has to be treated carefully, to avoid end-of-horizon effects whereby assets are only built for the final horizon and have no value for the rest of their lifetimes. Either model 20 years further (with lower intra-year resolution) or have a "salvage value" for investments made in the final investment horizon (I think OSeMOSYS had this).
BEWARE B): p_nom_max may become linked between different generators as potentials are used up.

issue_title: decide what to do with sparql support
issue_body:
eik...@gmail.com, 2010-02-09T15:24:06.000Z 
The sparql support currently has a bunch of issues and is in need of some major cleanup. We've 
recently removed a fair amount of unmaintained/unstable code from rdflib (all non core 
functionality and mostly store implementations). If we hope to release rdflib 2.5.0 anytime soon 
we will likely need to remove the sparql support (despite it being arguable more core than an 
arbitrary store implementation). 

If and when we get the sparql support into a more solid and working state we can include it back 
into rdflib proper. Perhaps if we get enough of the original authors helping out we can work 
though the issues in time for a 2.5 release like we did for the rest of the issues. Else let's work 
on the sparql support over in the rdfextra project.

The sparql support already plugs into rdflib as a plugin and so separating it into its own release 
should be straightforward.

Moving to rdfextra for the time being

non-existent) rdflib/sparql package ...

These issues involve bits that have been moved out of rdflib proper for now. We will re-open them 
or move them to rdfextas as appropriate.

See: http://code.google.com/p/rdflib/source/detail?r=1861

Thank you Chimezie for committing the your latest sparql code. There's a number of us that are willing to 
help stabilize the code and fix up the issues, but we were having a hard time getting our arms around the 
code. Ideally we can make a plan and all help get the sparql support added back to trunk.

I noticed the branch is called sparql2sql; does the implementation depend on the store being an sql one? I 
noticed a fair amount of sparql code in trunk depended on the mysql store related code when I was moving it 
out of trunk.

I'll update the Roadmap now and add a section for a sparql plan.
